Speaker 1: In this video, I'm going to be covering attendee communication and setting up a
platform for relaying the event details and bonding with your attendees before the
event and then also when they first arrive. Once you've filled your RSVP list with
attendees, you'll want to begin sending them specific details about your event.
Remember, just like Dean talked about in his LA Mastermind, you're looking to bond
and create a connection with all of your attendees. So although these emails need to
have event details and information that can be dry, find your own spin and way to make
them exciting, inviting and furthermore anchoring them into the reason they first said
yes to attending.
We'll provide you with a sample invitation that you can reference, but you'll want to be
sure that the email clearly states the following bullet-pointed items. You'll want to
include the dates, the times of the event, the location, and the room name if necessary.
Any suggested travel options included a hotel block link or code, if you do have that
option. What the weather's going to be like in that area so that they can be comfortable.
What to bring so that when they do attend, they're going to have the best possible
experience. And then any preparations that you want them to do before the event. So if
you have a course material or something that they need to review before they get
there, this can really help set a standard across the board so that everyone in the room
is at the same level.
Once you've sent the email, a quick tip that we usually suggest doing is sending another
form of communication. We typically use a text message to notify them of the email in
their inbox and this will just help with the open rate so that you can make sure each
attendee has opened the email and really looked at all of the details.
Another tip we've found is providing them with some form of a physical or printable
ticket for the event. This further solidifies their decision to attend and gives them
something tangible, otherwise know as tangible marketing. This is just contributing to
your brand and event recognition and growing your customers loyalty to the event.
You're going to want to set up a sequence of bonding emails. In the months, weeks and
even days leading up to the event, don't forget about your attendees. This is a mistake
that can easily be made as the focus is put on prepping for the event and planning your
event. In order to keep your attendees engaged, excited and committed to attending,
you'll want to create what we like to call a bread crumb trail. This is simply a sequence
of communications consisting of emails, text and notifications that spike their purpose
for attending and it can include sneak peaks of the event, releasing key speakers that
you're going to have and teach them, items that you can't wait to share, and you're
going to want to use these to anchor their decision to attend.
Throughout these emails, it's always helpful to include a link to the details of the event.
This will be the same information in your initial email with all of the event details. And
I'm going to show you what a reference looks like for that. This is one that we use for
our annual [Gain The Edge] event for Dean. You can see on here, it's just one long strand
of a website. It will have the dates, the location, where to book their room, where to fly
into. We also have a private Facebook group which can be an awesome way to network,
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

we're going to talk about that a little bit later. It can have some frequently asked
questions on here, which can eliminate some of those customer service things that you
may have to deal with, including questions about what items they will need to pay for,
where to check-in, where the hotel is, food around the area and just fun things to do.
About two weeks before the event, we suggest calling each of your attendees. We like
to call this communication a courtesy call. During this call, you'll really just want to
express how excited you are to see them in a few days, ask if they have booked their
travel and hotel accommodations and if they have any questions. This is another
amazing opportunity for you to bond with your attendee. If you'd like to add additional
questions to the call, feel free. Some examples that may help you in preparing for the
event and delivering the best content possible would include, what are you most
looking forward to at the event? And is there anything we can do to make this event
exactly what you're hoping for?
Another quick tip when you're making these calls, if you did choose to do a room block
with a hotel, ask your venue representative for the full list that has booked there. This
will help you know which attendees are planning to stay onsite and if they need to fill
the room a little bit more, you can suggest booking at that rate.
Once you're actually looking at planning the event, another way to bond with your
attendee is through gifts. Usually these are just tangible, small items that can be
impactful at the event. We typically do a favorite book from the expert or one of the
speakers, a journal and maybe a water bottle or a mug that's branded to your specific
style. Keep this in mind when you're going to plan the event and we're going to touch
base on this when we talk about the materials that you'll need to order.
Another way to bond with the attendees is going to be through networking. You can do
this easily by setting up simple mixers for the attendees and ways for them to stay
connected including Facebook groups, possible Zoom calls in the future, or even sharing
their information when they're at the event so that they can stay connected and really
begin to grow a Mastermind community between themselves. If you have any
questions, be sure to ask away in the private Facebook group and myself or our team
will be more than happy to answer.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com
